sourceTitle: Creating a knowledge hub by organizing docs for large teams

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of creating a home page for each team in Notion?

options.text: To store personal notes of individual team members
options.explanation: Home pages are meant for team‑wide information, not personal notes.

options.text: To centralize processes, best practices, and OKRs for that team
options.explanation: The article says a home page stores processes, best practices, or OKRs, acting as the team’s knowledge hub.

options.text: To replace the company’s external website
options.explanation: Notion home pages are internal knowledge hubs, not public websites.

options.text: To automatically generate meeting minutes
options.explanation: Meeting minutes require a separate database or template, not the home page itself.

questions.question: Which method creates a link to another Notion page while also generating a backlink automatically?

options.text: Copy‑paste the page URL
options.explanation: Copy‑pasting a URL creates a link but does not add a backlink.

options.text: Use the @‑mention shortcut and select the page from the dropdown
options.explanation: The article specifies that @‑mentioning a page creates a link and adds a backlink.

options.text: Drag the page from the sidebar into the current page
options.explanation: Dragging inserts a block but does not guarantee a backlink.

options.text: Insert an embed block with the page’s URL
options.explanation: Embedding shows the page but does not create a backlink.

questions.question: If you sync a section of a page across multiple locations, what happens when the content is edited in any of those places?

options.text: Only the original section updates; synced copies stay unchanged
options.explanation: The sync is bidirectional; changes propagate both ways.

options.text: Changes are reflected in real time across all synced locations
options.explanation: The article states that synced sections update in real time wherever they appear.

options.text: A version history entry is created, but the content does not sync
options.explanation: Version history is separate; sync ensures content updates.

options.text: Only the most recent edit is kept, older edits are discarded
options.explanation: All edits are merged; nothing is discarded automatically.

questions.question: Why might a leader prefer using a standardized Docs database over scattered individual pages?

options.text: It forces every employee to write in the same tone
options.explanation: Tone is not enforced by a database; the benefit is about organization and visibility.

options.text: It provides a bird’s‑eye view and lets employees quickly understand what others are working on
options.explanation: Standardized databases give leaders overview and help employees discover related work.

options.text: It automatically translates content into multiple languages
options.explanation: Translation is not mentioned as a feature of a Docs database.

options.text: It locks the content so no one can edit it after creation
options.explanation: Databases remain editable; locking is not a described function.

questions.question: Which combination of steps correctly restricts a page’s access and invites an external guest?

options.text: Open the Share menu → set access level (e.g., No Access) → click ‘Copy link’ and send it
options.explanation: Setting No Access would prevent anyone, including guests, from viewing the page.

options.text: Open the Share menu → choose the desired permission level → use ‘Invite’ to add the guest’s email
options.explanation: The article explains that the Share menu lets you set access levels and invite external guests.

options.text: Go to Settings → Members → add the guest as a member → they automatically get Full Access
options.explanation: Guests are invited per page, not added as workspace members, and they don’t automatically get Full Access.

options.text: Create a new database view → share that view only
options.explanation: Sharing a view does not control page‑level permissions.

questions.question: As an admin, which of the following actions can you NOT perform from the Settings → Members section?

options.text: Grant admin access to any team member
options.explanation: Admins can grant admin rights to members.

options.text: Create, edit, and delete groups
options.explanation: Group management is listed as an admin capability.

options.text: View all workspace guests and the pages shared with them
options.explanation: Admins can see guests and shared pages.

options.text: Change the content of a page that they do not have explicit access to
options.explanation: Even admins cannot edit a page unless they have been granted the appropriate page‑level permission.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to make meeting notes easily searchable and viewable for anyone who missed the live meeting?

options.text: Store them as PDFs attached to a random page
options.explanation: PDFs are not searchable within Notion and lack the database benefits described.

options.text: Create a Meeting Notes database and add a template for recurring meetings
options.explanation: The article suggests a Meeting Notes database to centralize notes and make them asynchronously accessible.

options.text: Post the notes in a Slack channel and link to the channel from Notion
options.explanation: While helpful, this does not place the notes inside Notion where they can be filtered and viewed.

options.text: Email the notes to each team member after the meeting
options.explanation: Emailing bypasses the central knowledge hub and makes future retrieval harder.

questions.question: How do headings, subheadings, and a Table of Contents improve the usability of a Notion page?

options.text: They automatically generate backlinks to every other page in the workspace
options.explanation: Headings and TOC help navigation, not backlink creation.

options.text: They break the content into digestible sections and let readers jump directly to the part they need
options.explanation: The article states that headings, subheadings, and a TOC make information more digestible and easier to navigate.

options.text: They encrypt the page content for security purposes
options.explanation: Encryption is unrelated to headings or TOC.

options.text: They sync the page with external documentation tools
options.explanation: Syncing is a separate feature; headings and TOC do not cause external sync.
